feedback.text: As someone who has spent years engaging with video gamesboth as a player and a researcherIm writing to share concerns about the way digital games are sold, maintained, and in many cases, taken away from consumers. The proposed Digital Fairness Act presents an important opportunity to address some of the unfair practices common in the industry today, particularly those that affect access, ownership, and longevity of purchased digital games. These practices increasingly affect people of all ages, but they are especially harmful to younger players and those who invest significant time and money into games they expect to own. 1. Removal of Purchased Games Many games sold as permanent purchases are later delisted, disabled, or shut down entirely. In some cases: Online servers are closed, even for single-player content. Games are removed from digital libraries after purchase. No offline options are offered, even when technically feasible. These practices render paid products unusable and leave consumers without recourse. 2. Misleading Ownership and Terms Games are often marketed using language like buy, own, or keep forever. In reality, most are governed by license agreements that allow publishers to revoke access at any time. These terms are rarely shown clearly before purchase, violating the principle of informed consent. 3. No Refunds or Support After Shutdown When games are made unplayable due to server shutdowns or technical restrictions, players are typically offered no refund or support. This is especially concerning when the shutdown occurs within a short time after launch or affects paid downloadable content (DLC) and in-game purchases. 4. Preservation and Access Rights Games are creative works that should not be lost forever due to business decisions. Without preservation rights or offline modes, entire titles and player investments disappear. This is not only a consumer issue but a cultural one. Recommendations: Require clear disclosure if a game relies on servers to function. Mandate minimum access periods (e.g., 510 years) for paid games. Enforce refunds or partial compensation when access is revoked. Prohibit misleading terms like buy or own unless full, lasting access is guaranteed. Require offline functionality for single-player content wherever technically possible. Encourage or require publishers to support preservation when deactivating products. Conclusion: The current model allows publishers to revoke paid digital games at will, leaving consumers without the protections they reasonably expect. The Digital Fairness Act should establish enforceable rights for access, refund, and transparency in the digital games sector. Consumers deserve clarity, fairness, and lasting value from the products they purchase.
